items: Analogue assignment relies on expert judgment and heterogeneous criteria, risking circularity and sampling bias; no sensitivity analysis shows robustness to alternative analogue definitions.

The landmarking protocol emphasizes canal and cochlear centrelines; cross-sectional canal thickness and absolute canal size—key functional variables—are not directly captured after Procrustes scaling, yet are discussed in interpretations.
PLS dimension 4 has a non-significant p-value (≈0.10); interpretations should be clearly qualified or supported with additional evidence.
The inference that developmental/genetic complexity causes higher evolvability is largely speculative and not directly tested.
Limited intraspecific sampling (appears to be single or very few specimens per species) may conflate species means with individual variation and measurement error.

Allometry and body size effects are handled indirectly (via a context variable); a formal size-correction pipeline (e.g., regressing shape on centroid size and/or body mass with/without phylogeny) is not presented.
Convergence assessment is primarily distance-based; it would benefit from established phylogenetic convergence tests (e.g., Stayton’s C1–C5, Wheatsheaf index, SURFACE/l1ou).
Coding of ecological variables (e.g., “agility,” “3D movement”) appears ordinal/subjective; no inter-rater reliability or sensitivity analyses are shown.

Precise details of CT resolution, segmentation protocol, sliding criteria for semilandmarks, and measurement repeatability are not fully described here; reproducibility would benefit from explicit reporting.
Terminology occasionally implies absolute size (“smaller canals”) while the configuration captures scale-free geometry; wording should be aligned with what the data capture.

Work on rapid vestibular shape shifts and plasticity in captive versus wild Mus musculus (e.g., the 2024 study summarized in the related work) is not discussed; it bears on the timing of inner-ear development and potential plastic contributions to interspecific patterns.

More explicit contrasts with univariate canal radius-of-curvature scaling studies and hearing-range datasets could triangulate functional interpretations of cochlear coiling and vestibular morphology.

The use of a single trophic enrichment factor (3.4‰) and a single moose δ15N baseline (from 2014 samples) to derive trophic position across 1993–2016 and across space may introduce unmodeled baseline/TEF uncertainty and spatiotemporal bias.

Maternal learning is proxied by the mother’s posterior mean trophic position (initially across years), which risks temporal misalignment with the actual rearing period and statistical “shrinkage” that could bias effect sizes; although a reduced analysis with observed rearing-year maternal values was included, this is not the main model.

Potential collinearity and identifiability challenges between maternal learning (fixed effect of maternal phenotype), maternal effects (mother ID random effect), and environmental similarity are acknowledged but could still bias variance partitioning.

The pedigree (16 microsatellites) and sample size (71 daughters, 33 mothers) may limit power to detect additive genetic variance; the “negligible heritability” conclusion should be tempered accordingly.

Environmental similarity is reduced to a Euclidean distance in a space of broad habitat composition variables with equal weights; key resource covariates (e.g., local moose density/harvest remains, ant abundance proxies, berry productivity) are not modeled directly, potentially underestimating environmental effects.

Year-specific diet samples are paired with a single home range composition per individual (median year used when multiple years are available), which may decouple diet and environment for repeated measures.

δ15N-based trophic position collapses dietary space into a single axis without accounting for multi-source mixing or routing; ants and moose having similar δ15N complicates interpretation of “carnivory” vs. insectivory.
The reported credible interval for the environmental component “9% [0.1–5%]” appears internally inconsistent (upper bound < point estimate), suggesting a reporting error.
Minor internal inconsistencies (e.g., environmental contribution sometimes 12%, sometimes 9%) and the questionable CI undermine interpretability.
The phrase “half a trophic position” for a range of 2.7–3.1 (≈0.4) is imprecise and should be reported exactly.
Work on stable isotope mixing models and compound-specific amino acid δ15N for improved trophic resolution is not discussed; these methods could address baseline and routing concerns.
A broader quantitative genetics context on the power and identifiability constraints of animal models in spatially structured pedigrees could strengthen the interpretation of low heritability.
Reliance on PROSAIL inversion for LAI and ALA without explicit uncertainty quantification; LAI saturation >4–5 and canopy clumping effects may bias estimates.

Stomatal conductance is validated indoors (gas exchange vs Penman–Monteith inversion) but lacks any form of field-level validation or surrogate (e.g., calibrated thermal imaging under controlled conditions).
Environmental translation of leaf width/length is demonstrated on a single hybrid for the environmental-response regressions; genotype-specific response heterogeneity is not explored.
Genomic prediction is limited to main-effect G-BLUP; no explicit G×E reaction-norm modeling that leverages environmental covariates to predict environment-specific trait values.
External validation sample sizes are limited for some traits (e.g., architecture on 20 recent hybrids), and some external validations show modest effect sizes (e.g., ALA vs predicted rhPAD).
The link from adaptive trait predictions to downstream agronomic outcomes (e.g., yield stability, water-use efficiency) is not quantitatively demonstrated.
Details on field spatial correction, UAV radiometric/BRDF corrections, and PROSAIL configuration ranges are not presented in this excerpt, limiting assessment of measurement rigor.
Methods for deriving BLUEs/BLUPs, spatial modeling of field heterogeneity, and cross-validation stratification to prevent relatedness leakage are insufficiently detailed in the text provided.
The crop model inputs and parameterization (Lacube et al. 2020) could be more fully documented, including sensitivity to input errors and uncertainty propagation.

Recent advances in probabilistic/physics-informed inversions of PROSAIL that report uncertainty (e.g., GP-based or VAE-based decoders) are not discussed; these could strengthen claims surrounding UAV-based trait retrieval reliability.
Broader connections to distribution shift methodology (e.g., WILDS-style evaluation thinking) are relevant given the indoor→field and field→field translations, but not leveraged.
Opportunities to benchmark or validate architecture retrieval with emerging 3D field pipelines (e.g., TLS datasets or 3D Gaussian Splatting + segmentation) are not explored.
